did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning algorithms (ResNet, GoogleNet, EfficientNet, VGG, DenseNet, MobileNet) and ensemble techniques (soft and hard voting) for the detection and classification of skin cancer. It also employs hyperparameter optimization to improve model performance on skin lesion datasets like HAM10000 and ISIC.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of an adaptive hybrid learning model for skin cancer detection. It details a specific methodology using deep learning algorithms (ResNet, GoogleNet, EfficientNet, VGG, DenseNet, MobileNet) and ensemble techniques (soft and hard voting) on datasets like HAM10000 and ISIC. The authors report new results, including accuracy metrics (82% for soft voting, 79% for hard voting) and hyperparameter optimization outcomes, which classifies it as a research paper rather than a review.

title: Adaptive Hybrid Learning for Skin Cancer Detection and Classification
journal: 2025 9th International Conference on Computational System and Information Technology for Sustainable Solutions (CSITSS)
brief_summary: This study proposes an adaptive hybrid learning framework for the detection and classification of skin cancer, including melanoma, using deep learning algorithms such as ResNet, GoogleNet, EfficientNet, VGG, DenseNet, and MobileNet. The model leverages ensemble techniques, specifically soft and hard voting, to combine predictions from these architectures. Hyperparameter optimization was applied to parameters including batch size, learning rate, and dropout to enhance performance. The approach was evaluated using publicly available benchmark datasets like HAM10000, ISIC, and DermIS. The proposed method achieved accuracy rates of 82% with soft voting and 79% with hard voting. The results demonstrate that integrating transfer learning with ensemble strategies and hyperparameter tuning provides a robust method for accurate skin cancer screening.

lesion_types_studied.details: Identified as the most deadly type of skin cancer in the introduction. Included in the HAM10000, ISIC, and DermIS datasets used for training and evaluation.
Included in the HAM10000, ISIC, and DermIS datasets used for model training and assessment.
Included in the HAM10000, ISIC, and DermIS datasets used for model training and assessment.
Referenced as the comparator class to melanoma/cancer in the classification task (e.g., 'chance of the cancer and non-cancer', 'malignant and benignant' images in HAM10000).

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic categories (cancer vs. non-cancer/malignant vs. benign) and specific lesion classes (melanoma, benign nevi, keratosis).
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly focuses on 'detection and classification' of skin cancer. The model predicts discrete classes using soft voting (probabilities for cancer/non-cancer) and hard voting (final categorical output). The target classes include melanoma, benign nevi, and keratosis from datasets like HAM10000 and ISIC. The primary task is binary/multiclass classification, not regression, as the output is a diagnostic label rather than a continuous numeric value.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A public benchmark dataset containing high-resolution, labeled skin lesion images. It is accessible to the general public and covers various skin lesion types including keratosis, benign nevi, and melanoma.

use.training: Used for initial training of the deep learning models (ResNet, GoogleNet, EfficientNet, VGG, DenseNet, MobileNet).
use.validation: Used for hyperparameter optimization and model assessment.
use.testing: Used for model assessment and evaluation.

datasets_used.name: ISIC
datasets_used.description: A public benchmark dataset of skin lesion images accessible to the general public, used as a basis for model training and assessment.

use.training: Used for model training and assessment.

datasets_used.name: DermIS
datasets_used.description: A public benchmark dataset of skin lesion images accessible to the general public, used as a basis for model training and assessment.

use.training: Used for model training and assessment.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the classification performance (true positives, false positives, etc.) of the proposed ensemble model using soft and hard voting.

To quantify the overall performance of individual base models (ResNet, GoogleNet, EfficientNet, VGG, DenseNet, MobileNet) and the ensemble models (soft voting, hard voting) on the training and testing datasets.
To assess the probabilistic predictions of the ensemble models on the training and testing datasets.
To determine the optimal values for learning rate, dropout, training/testing ratio, epochs, batch size, and optimizer to maximize model accuracy.
To convert probabilistic outputs from soft voting into binary classifications (cancer vs. non-cancer) using a fixed cutoff level.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, log loss, and confusion matrices without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.notes: The HAM10000 dataset was used for initial training. Hyperparameter optimization yielded the following optimal settings: learning rate 0.0001, dropout 0.3, training/testing ratio 80:20, epochs 10, batch size 16, and optimizer Adam. The cutoff level for hard voting was 60%.

ISIC.notes: The paper mentions ISIC as a benchmark dataset used for model training and assessment, but does not report specific quantitative accuracy results for ISIC separately from the HAM10000 results.

DermIS.notes: The paper mentions DermIS as a benchmark dataset used for model training and assessment, but does not report specific quantitative accuracy results for DermIS separately from the HAM10000 results.

architecture_used.main_model: Adaptive Hybrid Learning Ensemble (Soft and Hard Voting) of six pre-trained Convolutional Neural Networks: ResNet, GoogleNet (GoogLeNet), EfficientNet, VGG, DenseNet, and MobileNet.
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained weights from ImageNet were used for all six base models. The final classification layers were replaced with a sigmoid output layer and a dropout layer for binary classification (cancer vs. non-cancer).
architecture_used.input_size: Not explicitly reported (images were resized during preprocessing).

architecture_details.ensemble_method: Soft voting (averaging predicted probabilities) and Hard voting (majority class from rounded outputs).
architecture_details.feature_extraction: CNN models used for deep feature extraction; specific features mentioned include hierarchical fine-grained features (VGG), residual features (ResNet), reusing features (DenseNet), and balanced depth/width/resolution (EfficientNet).

training_details.optimizer: Adam (selected as optimal over SGD due to adaptive learning nature).
training_details.epochs: 10 (results stabilized after 10 epochs; tested up to 40).

classification_or_output_head.layer_modification: Final classification layer of pre-trained models replaced with a sigmoid output layer and a dropout layer.
classification_or_output_head.voting_logic: Soft voting averages probabilities across models; Hard voting selects the majority class from rounded probabilities.

preprocessing_techniques: Region of Interest (ROI) selection to isolate precise patterns
Data augmentation to increase dataset size

data_augmentation_techniques.details: The paper states that 'augmentation techniques are employed' to increase the dataset size because the set should be very large for the pattern to be learned. However, it does not specify the exact geometric transforms, color transforms, or specific augmentation methods used.

conclusions: The proposed adaptive hybrid learning framework, which integrates transfer learning with ensemble techniques (soft and hard voting) of six deep learning architectures (ResNet, GoogleNet, EfficientNet, VGG, DenseNet, and MobileNet), effectively detects and classifies skin cancer.
The ensemble model achieved an accuracy of 82% using soft voting and 79% using hard voting on the HAM10000 dataset, outperforming individual base models.

Hyperparameter optimization significantly improved model performance, with optimal settings identified as a learning rate of 0.0001, dropout rate of 0.3, batch size of 16, training/testing ratio of 80:20, and 10 epochs.
Integrating the advantages of multiple deep learning architectures through ensemble strategies creates a robust, scalable, and effective method for accurate skin cancer screening.

The study acknowledges that existing deep learning models often suffer from limitations such as high computational cost, limited generalization to unseen data, overfitting due to complex architectures, and a lack of diverse skin tone representation in large, unbalanced datasets.
important_remarks: The study acknowledges that existing deep learning models often suffer from limitations such as high computational cost, limited generalization to unseen data, overfitting due to complex architectures, and a lack of diverse skin tone representation in large, unbalanced datasets.

The paper does not report specific quantitative accuracy results for the ISIC and DermIS datasets separately, despite listing them as benchmark datasets used for training and assessment; results are primarily reported for HAM10000.

The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests; performance was evaluated using accuracy, log loss, and confusion matrices without statistical significance testing.

The proposed model achieved an accuracy of 82% with soft voting and 79% with hard voting on the HAM10000 dataset, which is lower than the ~92-99% accuracy rates reported in the literature review for other recent methods (e.g., Yang et al., Adamu et al.), suggesting potential for further optimization or comparison.
Data augmentation techniques were employed to increase dataset size, but the specific geometric or color transforms used were not specified in the text.

The cutoff level for hard voting was set at 60%, a specific threshold choice that may impact sensitivity and specificity trade-offs, though these metrics were not explicitly detailed beyond accuracy and log loss.
